data.title: More Information On The Gloomy American Consumer
data.description:
Greg Ip writes in the Wall Street Journal, "The Economy is Still Fine. Americans Are Still Gloomy."

He begins his article with this insight: "A lot has changed since November's election, but not this: The economy is still pretty solid, and people are still really down."

Mr. Ip uses this chart as the basis for his analysis.

My take on this picture is that the United States has faced a rising income/wealth gap in recent years and that this rise in inequality has permeated the economy and is spilling over into the election process, even to the extent of dominating the result.

That is, one of the major reasons President Trump won the U.S. Presidential Election in the fall of 2024 is that those individuals in the lower income/wealth classes were sufficiently dissatisfied with their position in the United States that they voted for Trump in the election even though the economy was growing at a relatively decent pace, and that unemployment was still near a fifty-year low.

As Mr. Ip explains, "The Economy is Still Fine."

But, "Americans Are...Gloomy."

My last post, "Wealth Effect: Credit Inflation Dominating the US" examined this outcome using a different data set.

What Mr. Ip has done with his article is just to provide more information that supports this conclusion.

The decade of the 2010s was a pretty good decade, with the economy generating the longest period of economic expansion in the post-World War II era. Economic growth was not outstanding, but it was solid and it was steady.

As mentioned above, unemployment was at a 50-year low and inflation was just a little bit above the Federal Reserve target for inflation of 2.0 percent.

Things were going pretty well and "consumer confidence" was steady, but the performance of the economy could not be called "robust."

The expansion carried up to 2020 or so.

Then the Covid-19 pandemic hit...followed by a recession.

Consumer confidence fell.

The Federal Reserve responded with a round of "quantitative easing".

Federal Reserve chairman Ben Bernanke introduced the Fed policy of quantitative easing in the early 2010s and had put the economy through three rounds of quantitative easing in that decade.

This policy was the "foundation" for the long expansion that took place during the decade.

In the early 2020s, however, the U.S. economy really needed some support. And the Federal Reserve responded to this need.

The economic recovery came about, but, the economic recovery was driven by a Federal Reserve policy that resulted in rising stock prices and rising real estate prices along with the prices of other assets.

The income/wealth gap increased...the bottom 50 percent of the income/wealth distribution did not keep up in this environment. The 10 ten percent accelerated their growing lead.

Consumer confidence fell, led by the sentiments of the lower levels of the income/wealth distribution.

Although not fully recognized at the time, this shift contributed significantly to the decline in the backing that Mr. Biden received in terms of the view of how he was doing his job.

This "drop" in "confidence" continued through the first three years of Mr. Biden's presidency.

Overall consumer confidence began to climb a bit going into the fall of 2024 and following the election results.

But....

But..., overall consumer confidence levels remained in the 70s.

Gloomy....

Really, gloomy....

This is not good!

And, now, consumer confidence seems to be dropping off for Mr. Trump.

Mr. Ip writes: "More respondents to recent polls for both Gallup and Quinnipiac University disapproved than approved (Trump's) handling of the economy."

This is not good, at all.

Confidence, overall, is not strong. And, although, as Mr. Ip tells us, "sentiment measures are lousy predictors," we still have to deal with the fact that "things are not fine."

Something has happened to the economy, and something has been reflected in consumer support.

The economy is growing...modestly...but there is a relatively large segment of the society that feels it is not participating in that growth.

They have expressed their views...in the recent election.

They now seem to be expressing their discontent in other ways.

But, no one seems to be responding to these needs with some kind of specific action. Therefore, one could expect that the needs will only grow as we move along the calendar.

Mr. Ip closes with the suggestion that "political leaders are vulnerable to a public that is deeply unhappy...."

Sooner or later, someone is going to have to respond.

The question is, how much of an economic jolt will be necessary to get a satisfactory response?

data.title: 9% Yield From Annaly Capital Has One Major Flaw
data.description:
We cover 18 mortgage REITs and most of the preferred shares associated with them. The largest mortgage REIT that we cover is Annaly Capital Management, Inc. (NLY). We actively cover three of Annaly’s preferred shares which are the following:

  1. Annaly Capital Management, Inc. 6.95% PFD SER F (NYSE:NLY.PR.F) (this is the preferred share I will primarily be covering today).

  2. Annaly Capital Management, Inc. PFD SER G (NLY.PR.G).

  3. Annaly Capital Management, Inc. 6.75% PFD SER I (NLY.PR.I).

Each of these preferred shares is currently paying a floating rate. We cover many shares that start at a fixed rate and then go to a floating rate - this is known as a fixed-to-floating preferred share. This means that at a specific date (specified in the prospectus), the preferred shares will go from paying a fixed rate to paying a floating rate. The floating rate is based on SOFR (which replaced LIBOR) plus a spread decided when the preferred share is first issued by the company.

For NLY-F the floating spread is 4.993%:

As you can see in the above chart created by The REIT Forum, NLY-F started a floating rate on 9/30/2022. At recent market prices, NLY-F is paying a dividend yield of 9.39%. In the chart above, the dividend yield corresponds with the column labeled "Floating Yield On Price." That’s a very attractive dividend yield, and it gets better when you take our risk ratings into account.

We assign risk ratings to all the securities we cover. As a general rule, mortgage REIT common shares should not be considered for buy-and-hold investors. They also shouldn’t be considered for investors who don’t have a moderate-to-high risk tolerance. Annaly Capital is one of the very few exceptions in the mortgage REIT space that we have given a risk rating under 4 (on a scale of 1-5). We’ve given Annaly a risk rating of 3.5 which means it can be an option (in tiny amounts) for buy-and-hold investors who have a medium-to-high risk tolerance in some scenarios. I still prefer trading them, though.

That being said, we assign the preferred shares from the mortgage REIT with a significantly lower risk rating. There are some exceptions, but that's the general rule. Most of the preferred shares we cover - along with some baby bonds - carry a risk rating of 3 or less. When it comes to preferred shares from Annaly Capital, we have assigned them a risk rating of 1. When we give an investment a risk rating of 1, it means that the security should be very stable, and it’s for investors who have a low-risk tolerance.

Given NLY-F’s high dividend yield and low-risk rating, some investors may be wondering why we haven’t assigned a buy rating. There's a glaring flaw with this preferred share reflected in the “Annualized YTC” column - also known as annualized yield-to-call. If an investor were to purchase shares today and Annaly subsequently called shares right after the purchase, the loss on an annualized basis would be -19.6%.

I’ll add another chart to give a clearer picture of how this works:

The worst-cast-to-call per share would be -$0.46. That would be a material loss if Annaly were to call shares right after an investor invested in NLY-F. Keep in mind that the high dividend yield on this preferred share is being paid by Annaly Capital. Because of that, there's the risk of the company issuing new preferred shares or debt at a rate lower than 9.93%. They could then use that new capital to call all shares of NLY-F. While this scenario isn’t certain, it's a material risk to investors buying in at this price.

It’s important to notice the progress to the next dividend. So long as an investor holds the preferred shares before the market opens on the next ex-dividend date, they will get paid the full dividend amount. That’s regardless of when they purchased shares leading up to said date. In the chart above, you can see we have $0.58 of dividend accrual already. When the company calls the preferred shares, it’s generally on a 30-day notice. Investors who buy on March 3 or later will not be getting the upcoming dividend. You're allowed to purchase shares in the after-hours of the previous day - before the ex-dividend date. However, if you purchase shares during pre-market on the day of the ex-dividend date, you will not get the dividend.

Final Thoughts

We cover mortgage REITs and most of their preferred shares. The preferred shares are generally a better option for investors who have a lower risk tolerance. We believe that Annaly Capital has some lowest-risk preferred shares (for credit risk and price volatility) in the sector. However, that does not mean investors should purchase shares with no regard for price. The key risk to consider here is that Annaly could issue a new fixed-to-floating rate preferred share with a lower floating spread. They could then use that capital to call all shares of NLY-F. We believe this is a material risk and would not be comfortable purchasing shares at the most recent prices. Investors who are currently in NLY-F should consider reallocating their capital elsewhere to reduce call risk.

data.title: January Personal Income And Spending (And PCE Inflation)
data.description:
Lots of income but not much spending in January – with inflation as expected.

Personal income jumped by 0.9 percent in January (about double expectations) while personal consumption expenditures (PCE) fell by 0.2 percent (markets were looking for a small increase). The weak retail sales report certainly suggested that the broader PCE measure would slow, but not by this much (with real PCE down at an annualized pace of 0.5 percent). Should we be concerned that these weaker spending numbers for January presage a significant slowdown in the economy (after all, the spread between the 3-month and 10-year Treasury note yields just inverted again in the past couple of days)?

At this point it’s much too soon to say that a downturn is imminent. The weak January figures came after strong fourth quarter spending (especially for December) and they may also have been impacted by worse-than-usual winter weather. Economic growth has remained above trend over the past year, so the optimistic view could be that taken together, the spending data over the past 3-4 months suggests that the economy is slowing closer to trend rather than stalling (or worse).

But what about inflation? After all, the Fed has often stated that its preferred inflation measures are the PCE and core PCE price indices. Both the overall and core (excluding the volatile food and energy components) rose by 0.3 percent, equal to market expectations. While expected, this monthly growth still translates into a 3.7 percent annualized pace – well above the Fed’s 2.0 percent long-term goal. But any one-month’s data can be volatile, and analysts (including those at the Fed) prefer to look at the trend. Despite these higher one-month inflation gains, the 12-month trend rates of both measures fell – with overall PCE inflation edging down to 2.5 percent and the core dropping to 2.6 percent (the slowest gain since last June). Still above the Fed’s goal, but moving again in the right direction.

Some analysts have raised the issue of Fed tightening this year, given increases in inflation over the last 4-5 months of 2024 and continued above-trend economic growth. Today’s figures suggest that the Fed will continue to be cautious about any near-term change in monetary policy until the trend in growth and inflation becomes clearer.

If the PCE figures in coming months suggest further weakness, we could see Fed easing by mid-year (especially if the PCE inflation figures continue to edge lower). But if the January PCE figures are a one-off and consumers continue to spend (and if the trend PCE inflation figures level off or move higher), it is possible that the Fed could tighten policy a bit this year. Most likely is that the Fed will keep policy unchanged for a while, with economic growth slowing modestly (but still probably remaining above trend) and inflation not moving higher (and hopefully moving a bit lower).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: NewAmsterdam Pharma: A Mispriced Bet On A Cholesterol Revolution
data.description:
NewAmsterdam Pharma Company N.V. (NASDAQ:NAMS) remains under the radar but has one of the best late-stage cardiovascular pipelines in biotech. Although most attention on the market today is focused on GLP-1s and their potential impact on cholesterol management, NewAmsterdam's obicetrapib - a CETP inhibitor with several successful Phase 3 trials - is a potentially game-changing drug candidate in lipid management. Its stock is underpriced in relation to its large cash reserves, strong clinical data, and differentiated commercial strategy. The skepticism is largely driven by the failure of past CETP inhibitors and the dynamic landscape of lipid-lowering therapies, but I believe these concerns do not take into account the superior profile and market position of obicetrapib.

Financials

The financial position of NewAmsterdam Pharma is solid relative to most clinical-stage biotech peers, but the market seems to discount its balance sheet strength. The company ended 2024 with $771.7 million in cash and equivalents and an additional $62.5 million in marketable securities - up from $340.5 million in 2023. That raise comes from a combination of follow-on equity offerings, milestone payments and option exercises that show management can raise capital effectively without excessive dilution. But despite this, the stock remains undervalued - perhaps due to persistent net losses that widened to $241.6 million in 2024 from $176.9 million in 2023.

Even at this burn rate, NewAmsterdam still has cash reserves to cushion the blow. The company has roughly three years of runway before it needs more funding if the spending is steady. But moving toward commercialization will likely mean higher costs - especially in sales and marketing. SG&A costs nearly doubled year over year to $70.4 million, reflecting early-stage preparations for the launch of obicetrapib. In contrast, R&D expenses slipped slightly to $151.4 million as some trials ended, suggesting near-term spending could stabilize before commercialization triggers a new wave of costs. The question is whether obicetrapib's clinical success will translate to investor confidence before another capital raise is needed. With a robust cash position and expected data readouts, I think the risk of dilution looks low now, but the window for execution is definitely narrowing.

Pipeline and Ongoing Trials

Obicetrapib is a CETP inhibitor targeting the lipid-lowering market at a time when GLP-1s are the most talked about. Three Phase 3 trials - BROADWAY, TANDEM, and BROOKLYN - reported positive LDL-C reductions with a clean safety profile. Such results counter-historical skepticism regarding CETP inhibitors - a class that saw high-profile failures at Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK) and Pfizer Inc. (PFE) due to off-target effects. Unlike its predecessors, obicetrapib is showing efficacy and safety, thus supporting the argument that it could have a strong position in cardiovascular care instead of being dismissed as a failed drug class.

Outside of these completed trials, the most critical catalyst remains the PREVAIL cardiovascular outcomes trial, which completed enrollment in April 2024. More than 9,500 patients are enrolled to determine if obicetrapib can reduce major adverse cardiovascular events necessary for widespread adoption and payer support. The first data from this trial is expected to arrive in early 2026, and obicetrapib's commercial viability definitely hinges, at least in part, on this outcome. Meanwhile, the ongoing REMBRANDT trial using imaging to assess the effect of obicetrapib on the coronary plaque may distinguish it from other LDL-lowering therapies. The VINCENT Phase 2 study is examining its effects on lipoprotein (a), an increasingly important but hard-to-treat cardiovascular risk factor. If successful, it would take the drug beyond traditional LDL-C management.

Clearly, their focus is on clinical progress, but the strategy of NewAmsterdam points to a broader commercial ambition. The company is not marketing obicetrapib as a direct competitor to PCSK9 inhibitors or statins but as a complement to those patients who remain at risk despite treatment. Such nuanced positioning could avoid direct competition with established players while satisfying an enormous unmet need. With multiple catalysts ahead using differentiated mechanisms - the pipeline is structured for long-term value creation - but the market has yet to price it ineffectively.

Pivotal Year Ahead: Clinical Wins and the Path to Commercialization

NewAmsterdam spent 2024 advancing the clinical potential of obicetrapib while providing the financial and operational foundation for its eventual commercialization. Most memorable was the release of topline results from three pivotal Phase 3 trials. Obicetrapib in patients with cardiovascular disease and HeFH showed a 33% reduction in LDL-C with statistical significance, while ezetimibe together in a fixed-dose combination in the TANDEM trial showed a 49% reduction. The primary endpoint of BROOKLYN which included HeFH patients was met, with a 36% reduction in LDL-C. Such successes established the efficacy of obicetrapib and also helped set it apart from past CETP inhibitor failures, the skepticism stemming from them is partially what weighs on its valuation.

The company also took key steps toward broader validation. Additional data from BROOKLYN was presented at the American Heart Association's 2024 conference, and NewAmsterdam said it planned to publish results from its major trials in top medical journals. Peer-reviewed publications won't necessarily change the direction of regulatory filings, but they may increase physician trust and differentiation in a highly competitive lipid-lowering landscape. A second highlight was the progress of PREVAIL, the company's huge cardiovascular outcomes trial involving more than 9,500 patients completed enrollment in April. If successful, this study may be further proof that obicetrapib does more than just lower LDL-C - it reduces actual cardiovascular events - an important finding for payers and physicians.

Besides clinical developments, NewAmsterdam is building inventory for commercialization - both U.S. and European launches if approvals come through. The company's large cash reserve helps with those efforts, but execution risks remain, particularly with payer negotiations and physician adoption. Several catalysts near term suggest that 2025 could be the turning point, but the stock is undervalued as investors wait for definitive proof that obicetrapib can turn clinical success into commercial momentum.

Risks and Competitive Concerns

In my opinion, the biggest hurdle is the historical baggage of CETP inhibitors. As I briefly mentioned earlier, Merck's anacetrapib and Pfizer's torcetrapib were potential blockbusters that were halted because of safety concerns or lack of meaningful cardiovascular benefits. Obicetrapib has shown a clean safety profile so far, but past failures promote skepticism, and it is harder to persuade investors and clinicians that this time is different. LDL-C reduction alone does not guarantee regulatory approval or commercial acceptance - the ultimate goal is the reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events. The true test will come in the PREVAIL trial, where doubts will persist until the results arrive. If PREVAIL is unable to demonstrate significant MACE reduction, obicetrapib's commercial potential will be severely limited. This would also reinforce the overarching narrative that CETP inhibitors are inherently flawed, making it difficult for the company to justify future trials.

A further challenge is related to the evolving cholesterol management landscape. GLP-1 receptor agonists like Novo Nordisk A/S's (NVO) Wegovy and Eli Lilly and Company's (LLY) Zepbound have reshaped cardiometabolic treatment and are now known to confer lipid-lowering and weight loss benefits. If cardiologists increasingly turn to GLP-1s for full risk reduction, obicetrapib may struggle to find a niche. Also, PCSK9 inhibitors with established efficacy in LDL-C lowering and cardiovascular event reduction remain strong competition, especially as pricing strategies evolve. Yet, scientific counterarguments imply that obicetrapib is uniquely positioned. Unlike GLP-1s, which are injection-dependent and primarily benefit metabolic patients, obicetrapib is an oral therapy targeting LDL-C reduction more directly. It also avoids some access and adherence issues with PCSK9 inhibitors, which remain underutilized due to cost and delivery methods.

Other risks include regulatory uncertainty. While these are impressive LDL-C reductions in BROADWAY, TANDEM, and BROOKLYN, the FDA and EMA will look closely at whether these translate into real heart attack and stroke reductions. Lipid-lowering approvals have historically been more stringent, given concerns that LDL-C reduction alone does not always correlate with cardiovascular event reduction. This is where REMBRANDT - NewAmsterdam's imaging study - may be of particular interest. If it shows that obicetrapib reduces coronary plaque, the drug's mechanism of action could become more compelling. Its financial runway protects some from near-term dilution, but investors will need to consider whether NewAmsterdam can execute efficiently as commercialization costs rise. Skepticism notwithstanding, obicetrapib has a stronger scientific case than most people imagine, and given its pipeline of upcoming catalysts, the narrative surrounding CETP inhibitors may shift significantly.

Valuation and Market Size

As a pre-revenue company, NewAmsterdam Pharma's valuation is tied to the market potential of its lead candidate, obicetrapib rather than traditional metrics. To get a better understanding of its market potential, I would look to the addressable market estimates.

About 1 in 250 people are affected by heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia, or about 1.5 million people in the United States alone. In addition to atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease cases, a significant proportion of the population has elevated low-density lipoprotein cholesterol levels despite treatment. In the US, the estimated candidate pool for obicetrapib, if we include HeFH and high-risk ASCVD patients, would be around 10 million individuals.

The treatment costs for lipid-lowering therapies are variable. Generic statins are relatively cheap, but advanced therapies like PCSK9 inhibitors are priced between $5,850 and $14,600 annually. If obicetrapib is priced competitively within this range, an average annual cost of $10,000 per patient is plausible.

From this, we can see that there is a potentially massive $100 billion annual market size for the United States. Even just a slice of this market could mean big revenues for NewAmsterdam Pharma. For example, achieving just 5% market penetration would generate $5 billion a year in revenue.

Outlook

NewAmsterdam Pharma is an uncommon case where robust clinical data, a large addressable market, and financial stability are accompanied by deep market skepticism. The company has steadily advanced obicetrapib through several successful Phase 3 trials, addressing efficacy and safety issues that have plagued CETP inhibitors in the past. But despite this progress, I believe its valuation does not reflect the upside potential - largely because of lingering questions about the drug class and the evolving cardiovascular treatment landscape. While competitors like GLP-1s and PCSK9 inhibitors continue to dominate headlines, obicetrapib's oral convenience and targeted LDL-C lowering offer a practical and strategic advantage that is not being fully exploited.

The most immediate inflection point will be the PREVAIL cardiovascular outcomes trial, which will determine if obicetrapib can disrupt the standard of care. Once these results are confirmed to reduce major cardiovascular events, there is tremendous commercial potential and the potential for regulatory approval and wider adoption by payers.

data.title: United Airlines: Fasten Your Seatbelts; There Could Be Turbulence Ahead
data.description:
Thesis

In the past six months, United Airlines Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:UAL) stock soared over 125%. In the below analysis, I determine that the technicals have turned highly bearish for UAL, with virtually all signals pointing toward weakness moving forward. The fundamental setup is currently much more attractive with earning results being resilient and guidance being quite strong in my view. I also determine that the stock continues to be undervalued, as the P/S ratio seems too depressed when looking at the rather solid fundamentals. Therefore, since the technicals are highly bearish, but the fundamental setup is net positive, I initiate UAL at a hold rating as there could be significant turbulence ahead for the stock.

Technicals

Charting

Trends

UAL stock was in a long-term uptrend from September to early February, when it broke below the trend line. The stock also started to form a near term downtrend that originates at the January peak in the stock. This downtrend line acted as resistance only days ago. Expect both the former uptrend line and this new downtrend line to act as resistance moving forward.

Support

There are two major support levels nearby. The first is at around 91 and represents significant support back in December as well as early January. The other support level identified above is at 80 and represents an upside gap that occurred in November. Even though the volume on that day was not extraordinary, this gap is the largest in the past six months, and so its significance is likely high.

Resistance

In addition to the two trend lines identified above, there are two more resistance levels for the stock. The nearer one is at 101. This level acted as resistance back in November and December, while being a floor for the stock from early January to just a few days ago. The other resistance area is at 110 and was a ceiling for the stock from early January to earlier this month, even though one bar was above this level.

Takeaway

I believe that chart is now clearly bearish for UAL stock. The long-term uptrend has been broken, a near term downtrend has developed, and including the trend lines, there is more resistance for the stock than there is support. Therefore, according to the chart, investors should expect further declines.

Moving Averages

Simple Moving Average (SMA)

The 50-day SMA had a bullish golden cross with the 200-day SMA back at the start of October. After the gap between the SMAs expanded by a large degree, the 50-day SMA seems to be stalling and as a result, the gap now seems to be in decline. This suggests that long term bullish momentum is now receding for the stock. The stock also recently broke below the 50-day SMA as another sign of weakness.

Exponential Moving Average (EMA)

The 9-day EMA had a bearish crossover with the 21-day EMA quite recently, reflecting the near term weakness in the stock. The gap between the EMAs is expanding, showing that bearish momentum is now accelerating in the near term. With the stock trading relatively far below both EMAs, expect this gap to continue to widen.

Bollinger Bands

The stock broke below the midline of the Bollinger Bands earlier this month as an indication of weakness. This also serves to confirm the end of the uptrend and the start of the new downtrend. Furthermore, the midline acted as resistance after this breakdown, and so that is further evidence that the downtrend is strong. Currently, the stock sits at the lower band and so an oversold rally could occur in the near future.

Takeaway

In my view, these moving averages clearly show a negative technical outlook for UAL stock. The SMAs show receding long-term bullishness, the EMAs demonstrate accelerating near term bearish momentum, and the Bollinger Bands helps to confirm the end of the uptrend and the start of the downtrend.

Indicators

Moving Average Convergence Divergence [MACD]

The MACD had a bearish crossover with the signal line in late January. The gap between the lines stayed small initially but has expanded significantly as of late, reflecting the increase in bearishness. The MACD also displays major long term bearish divergence. The stock made a major peak in January, but as you can see, the MACD has been weakening ever since its November high. This shows that while the stock continued to rally, weakness was starting to build.

Relative Strength Index [RSI]

The RSI is currently at a reading of 40.04 after a decline below the 50 level in the recent days. This shows that the bears are now in control of the stock by a healthy margin. The RSI has also been in a near term downtrend since mid-January, and so further weakness is likely at this point. Similar to the MACD, the RSI also shows major long term bearish divergence. The RSI has been on a weakening trend since its October high, while the stock continued to rally. This should have been an indication that the uptrend was on weak legs.

Stochastics

The %K broke below the %D quite recently as a bearish signal for the stock. After the gap between these line expanded significantly, there has been a slight closing of the gap as the %K line's trajectory improved. Nonetheless, with both lines plunging below the 50 level and the %K entering the 20 zone, the stochastics shows that the bears are highly in control of the stock now. Like the other two indicators, the stochastics has also shown broad weakening in the past few months, and so there were significant topping signs.

Takeaway

I would say that the indicators are overwhelmingly bearish for UAL stock. The MACD is in a bearish state, the RSI is in a downtrend and is now below 50, and the stochastics showed multiple signs of weakness. All three of these indicators also showed major negative divergence as the stock made a major high in January, but these indicators have been weakening for months, showing that the rally was running out of steam. Therefore, UAL is likely experiencing a major reversal of the long-term uptrend.

Fundamentals

Earnings

Quarterly Results

UAL reported their 2024 Q4 earnings back in January and showed resilient results overall. They posted total operating revenues of $14.695 billion, up 7.8% YoY and a diluted EPS of $2.95, up 63% YoY. As you can see in the chart above, revenue has been robust, but overall growth has not been overly impressive as of late. For EPS, while the current quarterly YoY growth rate seems quite impressive, as you can see above, EPS is not in a major long-term uptrend as multiyear growth has been minimal since late 2022. Furthermore, the revenue figure was able to beat expectations by $347.76 million while GAAP EPS missed by $0.15, showing that there was mixed performance versus estimates. Lastly, they reported that their pretax margin was 8.9%, up 3.2 points YoY showing that there has been an increase in the efficiency of the business.

Guidance

UAL provided guidance for their 2025 Q1 and the full year. They are expecting adjusted EPS to be in the range of $0.75 to $1.25 for Q1. Since adjusted EPS was -$0.15 in 2024 Q1, this represents a major improvement. For the full year, UAL expects adjusted EPS to be between $11.50 and $13.50. For 2024, they reported full year adjusted EPS of $10.61, which represented YoY growth of 5.6%. This guidance implies growth ranging from 8.4% and 27.2% which is quite a large range but, nonetheless, the midpoint represents meaningful acceleration in adjusted EPS growth for the full year 2025. Overall, I would say this guidance was rather strong as it shows that Q1 will likely see major improvement over the prior year quarter and full-year projections show that growth will pick up.

My Outlook

Overall my confidence in these guidance figures is moderate at this point, mainly due to two reasons. Trump 2.0 policies could have mixed effects on oil prices, but I expect the net effect to be lower prices. Even though sanctions on Iran are expected to lift prices, the end of the Ukraine war and 'Drill, Baby, Drill' policies are likely to cause softness in oil prices. This could help with UAL's margins significantly moving forward. However, moving to why I'm only moderately confident in UAL's outlook, it is mainly because the U.S. economy could experience growth issues if the Fed has to hold rates higher for longer. Most recent inflation readings have not been particularly encouraging and so therefore the economy may slow, leading to a slump in business for UAL. So therefore, overall, there are both reasons to be hopeful and cautious about their business outlook at this point.

Valuation

Price To Sales Ratio (P/S)

The P/S ratio is currently at above average levels relative to the past three years of data, but is still very low in absolute terms. Its current reading of 0.563 is a major expansion from mid-2024 levels of around 0.3, however. While growth is not particularly impressive currently, I believe the valuation is still too depressed to be justified. Q4's revenue growth rate was 7.8% and so this figure did demonstrate resilience. In addition, while UAL did not provide revenue guidance, it can be inferred that the significant adjusted EPS improvements in the guidance likely means revenue will grow meaningfully as well. Therefore, while major growth for UAL may not be present, the company is showing steady revenue results, and so I believe the current P/S ratio shows that investors are still unjustifiably pessimistic on the stock.

The Industrials sector's median P/S ratio is 1.47 and so UAL is trading at a major discount to peers. Of course many industrials are not airlines, but this nonetheless shows that the stock has a rock bottom valuation currently even after the multiple expansion. With relatively consistent results over the past few years, I don't believe this big of a discount is warranted.

Overall, I conclude that UAL is still undervalued at current levels. While the P/S chart above may not track the revenue chart that well, I believe this ratio shows undervaluation as the resilient Q4 revenue growth rate and likely stronger 2025 Q1 revenue growth rate should help to justify a higher multiple. Furthermore, the discount relative to the industrials sector is massive and so again, multiple expansion from here would not be surprising.

As stated earlier, the P/S ratio is not tracking revenue growth that well, but I believe UAL may be undervalued versus other airlines at this point. For example, key competitors like Southwest Airlines and Delta Air Lines have P/S ratios of 0.67 and 0.64 respectively. With the same macro factors like oil price and economic headwinds facing these airline companies, I believe it would be more reasonable for UAL to trade nearer to its competitor's valuations. Therefore, multiple expansion into the mid 0.6s would reflect a fairer valuation in UAL stock in my view.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the outlook for UAL stock looks mixed from here and so turbulence is expected. The technicals have been determined to be heavily bearish, as there were both near term and longer-term signs of weakness in the stock. The fundamental setup, on the other hand, is rather attractive as I believe the stock remains undervalued relative to the resilient financials and to the industrials sector. Therefore, the technicals and fundamentals diverge on the outlook for UAL. After soaring over 125% in the past six months, I believe it would be wise to now be cautious on the stock, and so a hold rating is likely appropriate at this point.

data.title: Best Buy: Juggling Tariff Risk, Consumer Weakness, Ecommerce Pressure (Downgrade)
data.description:
Best Buy Co., Inc. (NYSE:BBY) is going to report the company’s fiscal Q4 results from the November-January period pre-market on the 4th of March. While holiday season sales have shown strong signals for electronics sales, I believe that Best Buy’s uncertain FY2026 outlook may weigh more on the stock. Consumer sentiment was especially weak in February. Best Buy's market share losses to ecommerce competition and the major risk in a tariff on China also weaken Best Buy's earnings prospects going forward.

In my previous November 2024 article on the stock, “Best Buy: Q3 Outlook Remains Weak,” I initiated Best Buy at a Hold rating due to the company’s weak outlook, ecommerce competition, but a mostly reflecting valuation. Best Buy’s stock has since returned -2%, nearly in line with the S&P 500’s (SP500) return of -1%.

Strong Holiday Sales Signals Could Boost Best Buy's Q4

Best Buy’s Q4 report has interesting dynamics. In the company’s fiscal Q3 report, Best Buy lowered its FY2025 guidance in light of weak consumer spending, now suggesting mid-point Q4 sales of $13.72 billion at a wide year-on-year decline of -6.3%. The Q4 fiscal period includes one less week this year, causing an adverse impact of $735 million. Adjusting for the fact, sales are expected to decline -1.4%, slightly less than the -2.9% decline in Q3. The full-year adjusted EPS is expected at $6.1-6.25, suggesting a mid-point EPS of $2.38 in Q4, down -$0.35 year-on-year.

While Best Buy guides for sales to be down, holiday season spending shows more encouraging signals. Total electronics sales grew 3.7% year-on-year, showing stronger growth prospects for Best Buy than the lowered guidance suggests. Strong consumer confidence in December seems to have converted to healthy spending during the seasonally critical month. At the same time, though, industry-wide commerce data shows a continued shift to ecommerce during the holiday season, where Best Buy is very clearly losing to Amazon (AMZN) and others – Best Buy is very unlikely to reach industry-wide growth figures. The company’s ecommerce revenues declined in Q3.

Wall Street expects revenues of $13.70 billion and an adjusted EPS of $2.41 for the quarter, within Best Buy’s guidance range. The good holiday season sales could boost Best Buy’s financials above Wall Street’s expectations, but ultimately, the Q4 performance isn’t critical in the larger picture.

FY2025 Outlook Has Major Uncertainties

After better holiday season sales, Best Buy’s outlook could again worsen. Consumer confidence saw an especially large drop in February, contributing to a potentially much weaker consumer spending outlook.

Further, Trump’s proposed 10% tariff on China could weaken Best Buy’s outlook further. The company told in the Q3 earnings call that around 60% of the cost of goods sold are recorded in China, on which tariffs could be soon imposed. While Best Buy has moved the majority of controlled products out of Chinese manufacturing, the tariff on China is a major risk on Best Buy’s performance, also noted in the company’s latest 10-K. A tariff on Mexico could also impact Best Buy, as the country is the company’s second-largest source of imports.

Combined, the weak consumer outlook and tariff risks contribute towards a weak and uncertain outlook in FY2026 and beyond. Wall Street currently expects revenues to grow 1.3% in the year to $41.82 billion. With continued pressure from ecommerce competitors adding towards pressure in the year, even the slow growth expectation may turn out to be too ambitious. As a result, Best Buy may well set the FY2025 outlook below Wall Street’s expectations with the Q4 report.

Updated BBY Valuation: The Risks Are Not Worth Taking

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Best Buy’s stock for an updated fair value. I now estimate FY2025 and FY2026 growth to be weaker due to the aforementioned factors. Afterward, I estimate a rebound in growth from improved consumer confidence, but for the growth to fall back to just 1.5% as Best Buy is losing market share to better-adapted ecommerce competitors.

With a weaker sales outlook and tariff uncertainty, I now estimate the EBIT margin to stabilize at 4.2% compared to a 4.5% estimate previously. I still estimate Best Buy to convert earnings well into free cash flow.

The estimates put Best Buy’s fair value at $65.86, 26% below the stock price at the time of writing. Best Buy’s continued market share losses make the investment unattractive, and uncertainty around tariffs and consumer confidence add toward an uncertain and weak investment case. Potentially strong holiday season sales in Q4 aren’t enough to offset the risks, and I believe the risk-to-reward to now be poor ahead of Q4 earnings. Ultimately, Best Buy would need to position itself to gain better from ecommerce growth to be a more attractive investment.

The fair value estimate is down from $77.85 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital (WACC) of 10.53%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 4.55% interest rate, annualized from Best Buy’s Q4 interest expenses and debt. I again estimate a very low 5% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For the cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.23% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.33% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.49. With a liquidity premium of 0.2%, the cost of equity stands at 10.88% and the WACC at 10.53%.

Takeaway

Electronics sales were strong in the holiday season, but growth was weighed toward ecommerce, where Best Buy has lost to Amazon and others. The strong December could contribute to quite good Q4 financials after Best Buy lowered its guidance with the Q3 report. Ultimately, I believe that the forward outlook weighs more, where Best Buy may suffer from weakening consumer confidence, continued ecommerce market share losses, and a major tariff risk. The stock’s valuation has considerable downside due to the uncertainties, and as such, I have downgraded my rating on Best Buy to sell.

data.title: Intel: Battling Declining Profits, But Long-Term Bet Remains
data.description:
In the last few quarters, Intel Corporation (NASDAQ:INTC) was not a good investment for me. As I write this, I am rather close to breaking even and haven’t really lose money, but Intel is nevertheless not a good investment. Since my last article was published in early December, the stock declined in the meantime but especially in the last two weeks made up lost ground again and is now trading more or less for the same price. In my last article I wrote in my conclusion:

Intel clearly continues to struggle, and it takes a lot of imagination right now to see the light at the end of the tunnel and assume that Intel will be able to generate free cash flow again. I continue to see Intel as a long-term investment and a great business. And with almost every possible negative scenario priced in, the stock is undervalued and Intel a long-term “Buy”. The road ahead in the next few quarters might continue to be lumpy and the stock might fluctuate heavily. But I am very confident that Intel will be trading much higher in 5 or 10 years from now - and maybe the new CEO can even help to turn the ship around faster.

Let’s look at Intel once again and answer the question if there is hope after all. Intel has not found a new CEO yet, but there have been several news stories in the recent past justifying an update.

Quarterly Results

We start by looking at the last quarterly results – similar to many articles in the past. On January 30, 2025, Intel reported fourth quarter results and the company did beat estimates for earnings per share as well as revenue.

When looking at results on a year-over-year basis, net revenue declined from $15,406 million in Q4/23 to $14,260 million in Q4/24 resulting in a 7.4% decline year-over-year. And not only the top line declined, but operating income also declined steeply. Compared to an operating income of $2,585 million in the same quarter last year, Intel reported a decline of 84.1% and operating income was only $412 million in this quarter. And instead of reporting a diluted net income per share of $0.63 in Q4/23, Intel reported a diluted net loss of $0.03 in Q4/24.

We can also look at the full year results, net revenue declined slightly from $54,228 million in fiscal 2023 to $53,101 million in fiscal 2024 – resulting in 2.1% year-over-year decline. And while the company still reported a small operating income of $93 million in fiscal 2023, Intel reported an operating loss of $11,678 million in fiscal 2024. In fiscal 2023, Intel reported a net income of $0.40 per share, in fiscal 2024 the company reported a diluted net loss per share of $4.38.

Segments

When looking at Intel Products, revenue declined from $13,818 million in the same quarter last year to $13,027 million in this quarter – resulting in 5.7% year-over-year decline. And not only revenue declined, but operating income also declined 17.8% year-over-year from $4,414 million in Q4/23 to $3,629 million in Q4/24.

Client Computing Group revenue declined 8.7% YoY to $8,071 million in Q4/24 and Data Center and AI revenue declined 3.3% YoY to $3,387 million in Q4/24. Revenue for Network and Edge on the other hand increased from $1,471 million in the same quarter last year to $1,623 million in this quarter – resulting in 10.3% YoY growth.

And while operating income for the Client Computing Group and Data Center and AI declined year-over-year, Network and Edge generated an operating income of $340 million resulting in 212% YoY growth.

The second major segment is Intel Foundry, which generated $4,502 million in Q4/24 and compared to $5,175 million in Q4/23 this is resulting in a decline of 13.0% year-over-year. But the segment generated now an operating loss of $2,260 million in Q4/24 compared to $1,319 in the same quarter last year.

By the way, management mentioned that Intel will provide a new segment reporting beginning in fiscal 2025. These changes to the company’s segments will be announced with earnings for the first quarter in late April or early May 2025.

Good News For Intel?

The week before last, Intel gained 23.6% during the five trading days and while this was the best performance for Intel in the last 25 years, one can speculate what led to the great performance. In many cases, the answer is rather simple: Sentiment is driving the stock market and there is no major news story necessary for sentiment to change. Especially for beaten down stocks, such short rallies are not uncommon.

But in the case of Intel, one can also ask whether the stock price surge last week was driven by speculations about a potential breakup of the business. Rumors are that Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) as well as Broadcom (AVGO) are in talks about breaking up Intel and each business acquiring one of the parts. The news story broke during the weekend and at least in pre-market trading on Monday, investors seems to like the news with Intel trading about 5% to 6% during European market hours.

In my last article, I also wrote a little bit about Intel’s potential acquisition. I argued that an acquisition of the business was rather unlikely due to a market capitalization of $100 billion and when including a premium (which is almost always the case), there are only a few companies in the world being able to purchase Intel. Back then, rumors were especially about Qualcomm Incorporated (QCOM) acquiring Intel. But while Qualcomm has only a market capitalization around $200 billion, Taiwan Semiconductor and Broadcom both have a market capitalization around $1 trillion making it much easier to acquire Intel – especially when the business is split up in two parts.

During the Barclays 22 nd Annual Global Technology Conference, David Zinsner made a statement about creating a subsidiary for Intel Foundry:

Yeah, the only other thing I'd add is, we already run the businesses separately, but we are going down the path of creating a subsidiary for Intel Foundry as part of the overall Intel company. We're going to have a separate operational board for Intel Foundry, which is getting stood up today, will have its own ERP system and so forth. So that's already in flight. That's going to happen. And we're just a matter of time at this point that we lock in the milestones associated with creating that separation. As far as does it ever fully separate, I think that's an open question for another day.

Looking back at this statement with the speculation of Taiwan Semiconductor taking over a part of Intel, creating such a subsidiary might already have been a first step – but that is only speculation. I also don’t know how likely a breakup of Intel and acquisition is but we can state that Intel is not in a great position right now.

At the time of writing, Intel has an Altman Z score of 1.50, which is indicating troubles for Intel. A score below 1.8 is indicating a risk for bankruptcy with the risk increasing when numbers coming closer to 0. And when looking at the balance sheet, Intel had $3,729 million in short-term debt and $46,282 million in long-term debt on its balance sheet (last reported numbers from December 28, 2024).

When comparing the total debt to the total stockholders’ equity of $105,032 million, we get a D/E ratio of 0.48, which should not be problematic. Even when subtracting $24,693 million in goodwill (as these are not the best assets we can have), these ratios are still acceptable. We can also mention $8,249 million in cash and cash equivalents as well as $13,813 million in short-term investments on the balance sheet making Intel’s financial situation more or less acceptable at the moment.

The problem becomes obvious when taking into account that Intel is generating an operating loss as well as a negative free cash flow. This means that Intel is burning through its cash reserves or must generate cash in different ways – for example by issuing new shares. And while this is not problematic for a few quarters, it becomes an issue when Intel is losing money several years in a row.

At least it is good know that Intel will try to bring down debt levels in the next few quarters. David Zinsner stated during the last earnings call:

De-levering in 2025 remains a top priority for us on lower CapEx, increased cash from operations and value unlock across our non-core assets.

Panther Lake and Nova Lake

A second news story that should make us rather optimistic is Panther Lake, which is Intel’s lead product on the 18A node. During the last earnings call, management reassured that the launch in the second half of 2025 remains on track and Nova Lake will follow in 2026. Management is expecting that both developments will enhance margins and competitiveness for Intel again. Interim Co-CEO Michelle Johnston Holthaus stated:

Looking ahead to the rest of the year, we will strengthen our client road map with the launch of Panther Lake, our lead product on Intel 18A in the second half of 2025.

As the first volume customer of Intel 18A, I see the progress that Intel Foundry is making on performance and yields, and I look forward to being in production in the second half, as we demonstrate the benefits of our world-class design and process technology capabilities. 2026 is even more exciting from a client perspective as Panther Lake achieved meaningful volumes, and we introduced our next-generation client family code named Nova Lake. Both will provide strong performance across the entire PC stack, with significantly better costs and margins for us, enhancing our competitive position and reinforcing our value proposition to our partners and customers. Let me now turn to our traditional data center business.

And Intel will be able to directly compete with Taiwan Semiconductor as well as Samsung again. Taiwan Semiconductor is expected to begin mass production for its 2nm process in the second half of 2025 and Samsung will also start production in 2025. And although Intel is lagging a little bit behind, in my opinion it has the chance to catch up again.

Business Improving?

When listening to management during the last earnings call, we have even more reasons to be optimistic. Interim CEO Michelle Johnston Holthaus made the following statement:

We are also continuing to simplify our business and become a leaner, more efficient company. And most of all, we’re doing a better job of listening to our customers to ensure we meet their needs. Q4 was a step in the right direction. We delivered revenue, gross margin and EPS above our guide. Intel Products executed to drive revenue in the quarter, even as PC inventory continue to normalize. And Intel Foundry drove incremental operating efficiencies while achieving key grant related milestones, which supported solid upside to gross margins.

But Intel actually has to deliver on such statements. And especially when looking at AI data center Intel missed complete what customers wanted and is lagging other major semiconductor companies. During the last earnings call, Michelle Johnston Holthaus also stated:

We have learned a lot as we have ramped Gaudi, and we’re applying those learnings going forward. One of the immediate actions I have taken is to simplify our road map and concentrate our resources. Many of you heard me temper expectations on Falcon Shores last month. Based on industry feedback, we plan to leverage Falcon Shores as an internal test chip only without bringing it to market. This will support our efforts to develop a system-level solution at rack scale with Jaguar Shores to address the AI data center.

Overall, there are signs for improvement and several improvements we can hope for, but it all seems like a long shot. But there is one positive remaining – that is the stock price as Intel is trading for an extremely low stock price with a lot of negativities already included in the stock price.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

In the end, we could offer an intrinsic value calculation again. Usually, I start by looking at the P/E ratio as well as the P/FCF ratio. But with Intel reporting a loss per share as well as negative free cash flow right now, both metrics are not meaningful.

This leaves us with the option to calculate an intrinsic value by using a discount cash flow calculation. But especially when using a discount cash flow calculation, we must make several different assumptions and considering the current level of high uncertainty, I would like to repeat what I wrote in my last article:

The question we must answer now is the following: Is Intel in trouble, or is it a bargain? I offered several calculations in past articles, and I don’t want to offer another one. Especially as the free cash flow Intel might generate in the years to come is very speculative. When calculating with 4,292 million in diluted shares outstanding (the last reported number) and not assuming a major dilution, Intel must generate about $10,500 million in free cash flow annually from now till perpetuity to be fairly valued.

Of course, the free cash flow in the last two years does not inspire optimism about Intel’s ability to generate $10 billion in free cash flow annually. But when looking at the last two decades, Intel should be able to generate such an amount. It comes down to the question if we are optimistic that Intel manages the turnaround and will be able to grow again.

And I would argue in a similar way right now. We can make assumptions where Intel is deeply undervalued. If the company is able to successfully turn the business around, the current stock price is probably not justified. On the other hand, we don’t have much positive news for a turnaround already and if Intel continues to struggle.

Conclusion

In my opinion, Intel is worth the bet and the chance for a turnaround is enough for me to accept the risk-reward-ratio and I will keep holding Intel. And I will continue to rate Intel as “Buy” although I have been wrong several times with my “Buy” rating – at least so far. I remain confident that Intel will be able to turn its business around and Panther Lake as well as Nova Lake might be able to lead the business back to growth and profitability. Hopefully, Intel will be able to catch up a little bit in the AI race and offer products the market actually needs.

data.title: Pacific Basin Shipping Limited 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Pacific Basin Shipping Limited in conjunction with their 2024 Q4 earnings call.
